summary: Only one project-standards rule has a clear, evidence-backed violation introduced by this PR: erpc/networks_bench_test.go is no longer gofmt-compliant after the matchers conversion, which violates the explicit 'Format all Go files with make fmt' / 'Ensure make fmt and make test succeed before pushing' rule from .cursor/rules/erpc.md. Other concerns reviewers may surface (skipped tests, %v vs %w, code duplication between matchers/matcher.go and data/cache_policy.go, doc typos in matcher.mdx, nil checks on MatcherConfig, naming consistency between MatcherConfig and FailsafeConfig) are not codified as project-standards rules in CLAUDE.md or .cursor/rules/erpc.md and are therefore out of scope for this persona; they belong to other reviewers (correctness, maintainability, code-simplicity, doc-style). Pre-existing absences (e.g. func init/util.ConfigureTestLogger missing in erpc/evm_json_rpc_cache_test.go, common/config_test.go, upstream/failsafe_test.go) are not flagged because the diff did not introduce them and the new test functions added in those files do not themselves perform logging.

findings.title: erpc/networks_bench_test.go is not gofmt-compliant after matchers conversion
findings.rule_quote: Format all Go files with `make fmt`. ... Ensure `make fmt` and `make test` succeed before pushing your changes.
findings.recommendation: Run `make fmt` (or `gofmt -w erpc/networks_bench_test.go`) to fix the indentation of the inserted Matchers literal, then re-commit. Add a CI/local check that `gofmt -l` is empty before pushing as the standards file requires.

considered_but_not_flagged.topic: Conventional Commits style on feature branch commits
considered_but_not_flagged.reason: The branch contains commits like 'Refactor: remove unused matcher configurations...' and 'Enhance failsafe executor selection...' which start with capitalized verbs rather than lowercase Conventional-Commit types (e.g. `refactor:` / `feat:`). The standards file says 'Follow the Conventional Commits style ... when writing commit messages.' Most branch commits do follow it (`refactor:`, `docs:`, `feat:`), and this is typically resolved at squash-merge time on this repo's PR title; flagging would require reviewing the merged PR title rather than intermediate commits, which is out of scope here.

considered_but_not_flagged.topic: Missing func init/util.ConfigureTestLogger in test files touched by the diff
considered_but_not_flagged.reason: Standards rule: 'Always include func init { util.ConfigureTestLogger() } in test files that need logging.' The touched test files (common/config_test.go, common/defaults_test.go, upstream/failsafe_test.go, erpc/evm_json_rpc_cache_test.go, etc.) lack this init function, but they all lacked it at base 20402ad as well, and the new test functions added by this PR (TestMatchersValidate, TestFailsafeMatcherFinality, TestCachePolicy_MatchersIntegration, the new sub-tests inside TestNetworkConfigYamlParsing, etc.) do not themselves emit logs. Since the diff neither introduces the violation nor relies on logging, this is pre-existing and out of scope for this persona.

considered_but_not_flagged.topic: %v vs %w in fmt.Errorf wrapping (common/validation.go new lines)
considered_but_not_flagged.reason: The PR adds three error returns using `%v` instead of `%w` (`upstream.failsafe[%d]: %v`, `failsafe.matchers validation failed: %v`, `network.failsafe[%d]: %v`) while the rest of the same file consistently uses `%w` (e.g. `cache.*.policies.*.matchers: %w`). The standards file mentions `errors.As` for unwrapping but does not codify a `%w`-only rule. This is a correctness/maintainability concern, not a project-standards violation.

PR adds a `t.Skip("Behavior removed by PR #388 (matchers); see review comments #11/#12")` at the top of a ~600-line test function with a comment saying 'they should be replaced with new coverage ... before the PR merges.' This is dead test code shipped behind t.Skip, which is concerning, but the standards file in this repo does not codify rules against t.Skip, dead test code, or test-removal-without-replacement. Belongs to maintainability/testing-reviewer personas.

considered_but_not_flagged.topic: Code duplication: matchParam/paramToString duplicated between matchers/matcher.go and data/cache_policy.go (deleted in cache_policy.go but logically reproduced; cache_policy.go now also re-implements finality matching that matchers.MatchConfig already does for GET semantics)
considered_but_not_flagged.reason: Not codified in the standards files. Belongs to code-simplicity / maintainability persona.

Line 10 has an unmatched closing parenthesis: `- [failsafe policies](/config/failsafe))`. Line 13 says 'Method pattern using the syntax above' but the syntax section is below (line 100+). The standards file's only documentation rule is 'Documentation lives under the docs/ directory'; the new file complies with that. Belongs to a docs/style reviewer, not project-standards.

considered_but_not_flagged.topic: MatcherConfig nil-deref risk in matchers.MatchConfig and matchers.Match
considered_but_not_flagged.reason: matchers.MatchConfig dereferences `config` immediately (`config.Network`) without a nil guard, and matchers.Match passes `configs[i]` directly. Project rule about nil-checking is scoped to upstream and its config (`r.upstream != nil && r.upstream.Config() != nil`), not to arbitrary config structs. This is a correctness concern, not a standards violation.

considered_but_not_flagged.topic: Deprecation comment style for legacy fields
considered_but_not_flagged.reason: FailsafeConfig and CachePolicyConfig add a single `// Deprecated: Use Matchers instead` comment above a group of fields rather than annotating each field individually as godoc convention prefers. Standards files do not codify a Go deprecation-comment rule.

considered_but_not_flagged.topic: Naming: MatcherConfig vs FailsafeConfig vs CachePolicyConfig
considered_but_not_flagged.reason: The new MatcherConfig follows the existing `<Name>Config` suffix convention used by FailsafeConfig, RetryPolicyConfig, HedgePolicyConfig, etc. This is consistent with the codebase, not an inconsistency. The constants `MatcherInclude`/`MatcherExclude` match the existing CapWords convention. Nothing to flag.

residual_risks: Backward-compat surface area: legacy MatchMethod/MatchFinality fields and CachePolicyConfig.Network/Method/Params/Finality/Empty are retained but have shifted semantics (empty MatchMethod now means 'match all' instead of failing validation); behavior conversion happens in SetDefaults via convertLegacyMatchers. No project-standards rule covers deprecation timelines, but downstream consumers building FailsafeConfig in code (e.g. tests in this very PR) must remember to call SetDefaults / Normalize before relying on Matchers — see e.g. NewCachePolicy now calling cfg.SetDefaults() defensively. A standards-level note about 'always Normalize before consuming Matchers' could prevent future regressions, but is out of scope for this review.

Removal of per-method/finality default merging in UpstreamConfig.SetDefaults / NetworkConfig.SetDefaults is a behavior change documented inline as 'see PR #388 review comments #11/#12'. The standards files do not govern this; flagging belongs to architecture/correctness reviewers.
The shipped t.Skip on TestSetDefaults_NetworkConfig_FailsafeMatchMethod leaves no replacement coverage for the new matchers-based defaulting algorithm; this is a testing-reviewer concern.
testing_gaps: No project-standards rule mandates specific test additions beyond gock/init conventions, so this section is left to the testing-reviewer persona. From a standards angle only: the introduced TestFailsafeMatcherFinality and TestMatchersValidate do not require util.ConfigureTestLogger because they perform no logging, and they do not use gock so the gock setup-order rule does not apply.
